title: Design a Master Data Management System
description: Single source of truth for customer/product/material across 50 ERP instances. Golden record design, merge rules/survivorship, conflict reconciliation (LWW, trust hierarchy, manual), publish-subscribe distribution, consistency model (strong vs eventual), search at scale (Elasticsearch + HANA), governance.

sections.title: The Master Data Problem at Scale

description.value:
Master Data Management (MDM) is one of the hardest enterprise problems. When a global company has 50 SAP instances (acquired through mergers and organic growth), the same customer exists in multiple systems with different IDs, different addresses, different payment terms, and sometimes different legal names. A single customer might exist as "Acme Corp" in SAP US, "ACME Corporation" in SAP Europe, and "Acme GmbH" in SAP Germany. The MDM system must identify that these are the same entity, create a golden record, and keep it synchronized across all systems.

Scope: The MDM system must manage three master data domains: Customer (business partners, customer hierarchy, payment terms), Product/Material (material master, product hierarchy, valuation classes), and Vendor (suppliers, procurement info records). Each domain spans 50+ SAP instances and 200+ non-SAP systems (CRM, e-commerce, data warehouses). Total data volume: 10 million business partners, 5 million materials, 2 million vendors.

Key Challenges: (1) Identity Resolution — determining whether two records from different systems refer to the same real-world entity. This is a probabilistic matching problem, not a deterministic one. (2) Survivorship — when two records conflict (different phone numbers), which value wins? The answer depends on data quality, system trust, and business context. (3) Distribution — once the golden record is created, it must be distributed back to all source systems with minimal latency and guaranteed delivery. (4) Governance — who is allowed to modify master data? What is the approval workflow? How do we audit changes?

decisionTree.firstQuestion: "Is this master data domain centralized or distributed?" Centralized MDM (single source of truth with golden record) requires deterministic identity resolution first, probabilistic second. Distributed MDM (each system owns its data, hub synchronizes) requires conflict reconciliation with trust scoring.

branches.condition: Single source of truth required with strong governance (Customer, Vendor)
branches.path: Centralized MDM hub with golden record. Identity resolution pipeline: deterministic (tax ID/DUNS) -> probabilistic (fuzzy name/address) -> ML-based (relationship graph). Golden record synthesized from highest-trust sources.

branches.condition: Distributed ownership with hub-based synchronization (Material, Product)
branches.path: Distributed MDM with publish-subscribe synchronization. Each source system owns its data. Hubs maintains cross-references and resolves conflicts via trust hierarchy. Higher operational complexity but preserves local autonomy.

branches.condition: Hybrid: core attributes centralized, extended attributes distributed
branches.path: Hub-and-spoke with attribute-level ownership. Core attributes (tax ID, legal name) owned by hub. Extended attributes (marketing description, regional variants) owned by source systems with LWW conflict resolution.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: The centralized vs distributed decision is not about technology — it's about organizational power. Centralized MDM requires a strong central governance team. Distributed MDM requires more technical infrastructure but less organizational change. At Staff+, recommend based on the org's data maturity, not technical purity.

organizationalDimension.migrationStrategy: Phased rollout using the 'Trusted Source' pattern: start with one domain (Customer) and one high-quality source (SAP S/4HANA US). Build the golden record for that source first, then incrementally add sources. Phase 1 (months 1-3): onboard SAP US customer data, establish deterministic matching. Phase 2 (months 4-6): add SAP EU and APAC, implement probabilistic matching. Phase 3 (months 7-12): add non-SAP sources (CRM, e-commerce), implement ML matching and conflict resolution.

stakeholders.concern: Our data will be overwritten by the golden record. We lose control over 'our' customers.
stakeholders.address: Source systems retain their local data and IDs. The golden record is a separate entity. Source systems continue to operate independently. The golden record adds a cross-reference layer — it does not replace source data. Source system owners set the trust score for their data.

stakeholders.concern: Identity resolution is probabilistic — there will be false positives (wrong merges) and false negatives (missed matches).
stakeholders.address: The matching pipeline uses confidence thresholds. Matches below 90% confidence are routed to the manual reconciliation queue — no automatic merge at low confidence. Data stewards review and either confirm or reject. False positives trigger a 'split' workflow that reverts the merge and updates the matching algorithm.

stakeholders.concern: Golden record will create process overhead. We will need to approve changes we don't care about.
stakeholders.address: Only critical attributes (tax ID, legal name, credit limit) require approval. Non-critical attributes (phone, email, shipping address) use auto-merge with provenance — all values are preserved, and consumers choose which to use.

description.value:
The golden record is the system of record for master data. It is not a copy of any source system's data — it is a synthesized record that represents the best-known truth about an entity, derived from multiple source systems through identity resolution and survivorship rules.

Golden Record Schema: Each golden record has three layers. Layer 1: Core Attributes (immutable identifiers — Golden Record ID, creation timestamp, last modified, version number). Layer 2: Domain Attributes (customer-specific: name, address, tax ID; product-specific: description, category, UOM; vendor-specific: payment terms, procurement group). Layer 3: Metadata (source system IDs mapping — SAP_US:BP-12345, SAP_EU:BP-67890, CRM:CUST-ABCDE; trust score; last synchronized timestamp per source; change history).

Identity Resolution Pipeline: The resolution process has three stages. Stage 1: Deterministic Matching — exact matches on known identifiers (Tax ID, DUNS number, GLN). If Tax ID matches, it is 99.9%% likely the same entity. This catches 40% of matches. Stage 2: Probabilistic Matching — fuzzy matching on name, address, phone, email using algorithms like Jaro-Winkler distance (for names), Levenshtein distance (for addresses), and Soundex (for phonetic matching). This catches 35% more matches with >80%% confidence. Stage 3: ML-Based Matching — a trained model using features like name similarity, address co-occurrence, email domain overlap, and relationship graph analysis. This catches the remaining 25% of matches, including edge cases like name changes after mergers.

Trust Scoring: Each source system has a trust score (0.0–1.0) based on historical data quality. SAP S/4HANA with strict validation might have trust 0.95. A legacy CRM with no validation might have trust 0.60. The golden record's per-attribute trust score is the weighted average of all source values, weighted by source trust. When trust falls below 0.80 for an attribute, the record is flagged for manual stewardship.

cp.monthlyPenalty: $45,000 match confidence > 95%: lowers false positives but 25% of records go to manual review, requiring 15 stewards at $3K/month each

ap.monthlyPenalty: $28,000 match confidence > 80%: auto-merges more records (only 10% manual review) but risks 3% false positive rate requiring quarterly reconciliation sweeps

calculation.breakeven: At 10M customer records, the high-confidence strategy costs 1.6x more in steward headcount but reduces false positives by 5x. The breakeven is at 5M records — below that, the cost of false positives (rework, customer impact) exceeds the steward cost savings.

description.value:
When two source records conflict on an attribute value, the MDM system must determine which value to include in the golden record. There is no single correct answer — the right strategy depends on the attribute, the source quality, and the business context. The MDM system supports multiple reconciliation strategies configurable per attribute.

Strategy 1: Last-Writer-Wins (LWW). The golden record takes the value from the source that most recently updated the attribute. This is simple to implement and works well for attributes that change frequently (phone number, email address). Risk: a low-quality source that recently updated with incorrect data can overwrite a correct value from a high-quality source. Mitigation: only apply LWW for sources with trust > 0.80.

Strategy 2: Trust Hierarchy. Each source has a trust score. When sources disagree, the value from the highest-trust source wins. This is the default strategy for critical attributes (Tax ID, Legal Name, Credit Limit). Implementation: a configurable Source Trust Table that ranks sources by domain. For Customer Credit Limit: S/4HANA FI (trust 1.0) > CRM (trust 0.8) > Data Warehouse (trust 0.5). The highest-trust source with a non-null value determines the golden record value.

Strategy 3: Merge with Provenance. For attributes where multiple values are valid (phone numbers, email addresses, shipping addresses), the golden record stores ALL values with provenance metadata. Each value is tagged with: source system, last updated timestamp, confidence score, and reason code (primary, secondary, deprecated). Consumers can choose to use the primary value or all values. This is the most flexible but most complex strategy.

Strategy 4: Manual Reconciliation Workflow. When automated strategies cannot reach sufficient confidence (< 80%), the record is routed to a manual data steward for review. The steward sees: conflicting records side-by-side with highlighted differences, source trust scores, last updated timestamps, and a recommendation from the auto-resolution engine. The steward selects the winning value or creates a composite. All manual decisions are logged for audit and used as training data for the ML matching model.

review</text><text class="mr-sub" x="525" y="183">Logged for audit, used as ML training data</text><rect class="mr-box" x="20" y="210" width="660" height="120"/><text class="mr-lbl" x="350" y="230">Decision Tree for Conflict Resolution</text><text class="mr-sub" x="350" y="250">Is this a single-value attribute? → Yes → Choose strategy 1 or 2</text><text class="mr-sub" x="350" y="266">Is this a multi-value attribute? → Yes → Choose strategy 3 (merge)</text><text class="mr-sub" x="350" y="282">Is automated confidence < 80%? → Yes → Route to strategy 4 (manual)</text><text class="mr-sub" x="350" y="302">Source Trust Table Example: S/4 FI (1.0) > CRM (0.8) > DW (0.5)</text><text class="mr-sub" x="350" y="320">All strategy decisions logged in immutable audit trail for SOX compliance</text></svg>

Once the golden record is created or updated, it must be distributed to all 250+ source systems (50 SAP + 200 non-SAP). The distribution mechanism must handle: variable message sizes (a customer golden record may be 50KB with attachments), variable recipient capabilities (SAP systems speak IDoc/RFC, non-SAP systems speak REST/gRPC), and guaranteed delivery with exactly-once semantics.

Distribution Topology: The MDM hub uses a publish-subscribe model with an event bus (Kafka). When a golden record changes, the MDM hub publishes a GoldenRecordChanged event to the mdm.golden-record.changed topic. Each source system subscribes to this topic via a dedicated consumer group (one per system). The consumer has a subscription filter that matches only records relevant to that system (e.g., SAP US only receives records where it is listed as a source). This avoids flooding systems with irrelevant data.

Distribution Connectors: Each target system has a distribution connector that: (1) receives the CloudEvent from Kafka, (2) maps the golden record to the target system's format (SAP IDoc, Salesforce REST object, custom JSON), (3) invokes the target system's API with the mapped data, (4) handles success (commits the offset) or failure (retries or DLQ). The connector is stateless (all state is in Kafka offsets) and can be scaled horizontally.

Incremental vs. Full Load: Initial load for a new system is a full data dump (all golden records). The full load uses a parallel streaming approach: the MDM hub exports records to Parquet files in object storage, the target system bulk-loads the files, and then the hub starts incremental distribution via Kafka. The cutover from full load to incremental is managed by a watermark offset: the hub records the offset of the last full-load event, and incremental events start from that offset.

decisionTree.firstQuestion: "Does the consumer need real-time updates or is batch-within-SLA acceptable?" Real-time consumers (order processing, credit checks) need pub-sub with sub-second latency. Batch consumers (data warehouse, reporting) can accept hourly or daily snapshots.

branches.condition: Consumer needs sub-second updates (transactional systems, credit checks)
branches.path: Event-driven pub-sub via Kafka. GoldenRecordChanged events published to mdm.golden-record.changed topic. Each consumer maintains its own offset. Exactly-once delivery with idempotent consumers. Latency: < 1 second.

branches.condition: Consumer accepts hourly/daily updates (data warehouse, analytics)
branches.path: Batch distribution via scheduled snapshots. Golden records exported to Parquet files in object storage. Consumer pulls via scheduled ETL. Simpler and cheaper — no Kafka infrastructure needed. Latency: 1-24 hours.

branches.condition: Consumer is an SAP system with IDoc interface
branches.path: Hybrid: events trigger immediate IDoc generation for critical attributes (tax ID, credit limit). Full material master replication via batch IDoc for non-critical attributes. SAP's ALE distribution model handles the routing.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: Do not propose Kafka for all distribution. Batch distribution via S3/Parquet is 10x cheaper and operationally simpler for consumers that don't need real-time data. The Staff+ answer maps distribution mechanism to consumer latency requirement, not to technology preference.

description.value:
The MDM system must balance two competing requirements: strong consistency for governance (you cannot distribute a golden record until it has been approved) and low latency for search (users expect sub-second results). We address this with a command-query separation architecture: a strongly consistent write path and an eventually consistent read/search path.

Write Path (Strong Consistency): Golden record writes go through the MDM Hub via a transactional API. The flow: (1) Source system submits a change request via REST API (synchronous). (2) The MDM hub validates the request, applies conflict resolution rules, and updates the golden record in HANA (ACID transaction). (3) The update is logged in the change audit table. (4) The MDM hub publishes a GoldenRecordChanged event to Kafka (transactional outbox pattern — DB update and event publication are atomic). (5) The REST API returns success to the caller with the updated golden record. All of this happens synchronously within a single transaction. Latency target: P99 under 500ms for the write path.

Read/Search Path (Eventual Consistency): Search queries go through Elasticsearch, not HANA. The index is updated asynchronously via Kafka → Logstash → Elasticsearch pipeline. When a golden record is updated, the Kafka event is consumed by the indexing pipeline, which updates the Elasticsearch index. There is a 1–5 second delay between the write and the index update. This is acceptable because search is a discovery tool — users searching for a customer expect to find it, but a 2-second delay for the most recent update is tolerable. For read-by-ID (where consistency matters), the read path goes directly to HANA.

Elasticsearch + HANA Coexistence: HANA is the system of record; Elasticsearch is the search engine. The data model in Elasticsearch is denormalized for search: each golden record is a single Elasticsearch document with all attributes flattened and indexed for full-text search. The Elasticsearch mapping includes: text fields with language-specific analyzers (English, German, French, Chinese), keyword fields for faceted search (by country, by customer group, by status), and geo-point fields for location-based search. The HANA-Elasticsearch sync is monitored for lag; if lag exceeds 30 seconds, an alert is triggered and the indexing pipeline is scaled.

<svg viewBox="0 0 700 380" adversarialResponse.challenge: "Your search for BOM and material masters returns results that are 1-5 seconds stale. Our warehouse operators need real-time material availability. Why not use strong consistency for search too?"
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "Strong consistency would make the write path too slow. Eventual consistency is standard for search." (dismissive, doesn't address the operational need)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "You are right that 1-5 second staleness is unacceptable for warehouse operations where real-time material availability determines whether an order can be fulfilled. Let me address this with a read-path separation: the search index serves discovery queries (find material by description, find customer by name) where 1-5 second staleness is acceptable. For real-time material availability (is this BOM component in stock?), the read path goes directly to HANA — not Elasticsearch. HANA returns the current committed balance with sub-10ms latency because it reads from the same ACID transaction store. The search index is only for full-text discovery, not for transactional reads. The key design point: we use the CQRS pattern where 'read' is split into 'search' (eventually consistent, Elasticsearch) and 'lookup' (strongly consistent, HANA via REST API). The warehouse operator UI calls the lookup API for material availability, not the search API. This gives us sub-second latency for critical operational queries while maintaining the cost and performance benefits of eventual consistency for discovery queries. The additional infrastructure cost: none — HANA for lookup queries is already running. We just expose a different API endpoint."
adversarialResponse.pattern: separate-concerns — acknowledge the valid constraint, distinguish between 'search' (discovery, eventual consistency OK) and 'lookup' (transactional, strong consistency required), propose a read-path separation that requires no additional infrastructure. The Staff+ insight is that not all reads are the same.

description.value:
Governance is what separates an MDM system from a data mess. Without governance, golden records accumulate conflicts, trust scores degrade, and the system becomes as unreliable as the source systems it was meant to clean. Governance covers: who can do what, what approval workflows are required, how changes are audited, and how data quality is measured and improved.

Role Model: Four roles in the MDM governance model. Data Steward — resolves conflicts in manual reconciliation queue, approves or rejects merge proposals, handles exception cases. Data Owner — responsible for data quality of a domain (Customer Data Owner, Product Data Owner), defines trust scores and survivorship rules for their domain, approves schema changes. System Owner — responsible for a source system's integration with MDM, manages the distribution connector, monitors sync health. MDM Administrator — manages the platform, configures matching algorithms, monitors system performance, handles escalations.

Change Approval Workflows: Not all changes require approval. Changes are classified: Auto-Approved (trust score > 0.90, non-critical attribute, no active conflict), Steward-Approved (trust score 0.70–0.90 or critical attribute change like Tax ID), Data-Owner-Approved (new entity creation, merge of two existing golden records), Admin-Approved (schema changes, trust score modifications, system onboarding). The workflow engine is configurable per domain and supports parallel approval, escalation after timeout (48h), and delegation.

Audit Trail: Every state change to a golden record is recorded in an immutable audit log. The audit event includes: actor (user or system), action (CREATE, UPDATE, MERGE, SPLIT, APPROVE, REJECT), timestamp, before-state (full record snapshot), after-state (full record snapshot), reason code, and workflow ID (if part of approval). The audit log is stored in a separate HANA partition (append-only, no updates) and retained for 10 years for SOX compliance. Audit queries are exposed via a search UI for compliance officers.

organizationalDimension.migrationStrategy: The MDM governance model is rolled out in three waves aligned with the system deployment. Wave 1 (months 1-3): standing up the MDM Center of Excellence with the Data Steward and Data Owner roles for Customer domain. Wave 2 (months 4-8): adding Product/Material and Vendor domains, onboarding System Owners for each source system. Wave 3 (months 9-12): full governance operating model with automated quality scorecards, escalation workflows, and monthly governance review boards.

stakeholders.concern: MDM governance spans 250+ source systems, but we only have budget for 15 people.
stakeholders.address: Governance is tiered by data criticality. Tier 1 (financial-impact data: tax IDs, credit limits, legal names) has full human governance with data stewards. Tier 2 (operational data: addresses, phone numbers) uses automated governance with exception-based human review. Tier 3 (informational data: marketing preferences, descriptions) is fully automated. This 3-tier model covers 100% of data with 15 FTEs.

stakeholders.concern: Manual reconciliation is tedious. We need better tooling to reduce repetitive work.
stakeholders.address: The reconciliation UI shows suggested merges with confidence scores. Stewards approve or reject with one click. Bulk operations allow approving 50+ similar conflicts at once. Automated learning: when a steward rejects a suggested merge, the matching algorithm is updated to reduce similar false positives in the future.

stakeholders.concern: We need different quality thresholds for different domains. Finance needs 99.9% accuracy; Marketing can tolerate 95%.
stakeholders.address: Quality thresholds are configurable per domain. Finance domain: minimum trust score of 0.95 for auto-approve. Marketing domain: minimum trust score of 0.80. Each domain owner sets their own thresholds within the MDM governance framework.

details: Staff+ Insight: MDM is a favorite FAANG system design topic because it combines data modeling, distributed systems, ML (matching), and governance — all Staff+ concerns. The key is to show you understand that MDM is not just a technical problem. The identity resolution algorithm, the conflict reconciliation strategy, and the governance model are all equally important. Most candidates focus on the technology. Staff+ candidates focus on the data quality flywheel: better matching → better golden records → better source systems → better matching.
